title: Error boundaries and recovery

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Error boundaries and recovery; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Error boundaries and recovery
sources.claim: React error boundaries catch render-tree errors below them and show recovery UI, while async, event, and server errors need their own handling.

canonicalExplanation.summary: React error boundaries catch render-tree errors below them and show recovery UI, while async, event, and server errors need their own handling.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A route boundary reports a safe incident id and lets the learner retry without exposing stack details. Your goal is to design and test a route or feature error boundary with useful fallback and reset behavior. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Routing and application context; Remote data and asynchronous UI; Loading, empty, success and failure states; javascript errors exceptions. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: React error boundaries catch render-tree errors below them and show recovery UI, while async, event, and server errors need their own handling. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Boundaries do not catch their own errors, reset strategy matters, and logging must avoid sensitive data. This draft therefore rejects: One error boundary catches rejected fetches and every event-handler exception automatically.

includes: Contain render failures at meaningful regions and provide recovery without masking event or async errors.
Design and test a route or feature error boundary with useful fallback and reset behavior.
excludes: Boundaries do not catch their own errors, reset strategy matters, and logging must avoid sensitive data.

outcomes: Design and test a route or feature error boundary with useful fallback and reset behavior.

examples.title: Error boundaries and recovery: concrete case
examples.situation: A route boundary reports a safe incident id and lets the learner retry without exposing stack details.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A route boundary reports a safe incident id and lets the learner retry without exposing stack details.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Error boundaries cover descendant rendering failures; handle async and event errors at their actual boundaries.
examples.result: The result follows because Error boundaries cover descendant rendering failures; handle async and event errors at their actual boundaries.

Step 3 — Reject the misconception: One error boundary catches rejected fetches and every event-handler exception automatically.

limitations: A workshop does not reproduce reconciliation identity, hook ordering, concurrent rendering, effects, or browser accessibility APIs.
